original_abstract: This article explores the landscape of educational inclusion for students with special needs, framing the issue within the broader context of human rights and contemporary public policy. Utilizing a comprehensive bibliographic review of empirical studies and international reports, the research evaluates the core obstacles and potential growth areas for inclusive pedagogical practices, particularly in Latin American settings. The findings indicate that system-wide implementation is frequently hindered by fiscal constraints, inadequate physical infrastructure, and a significant gap in specialized teacher training. Through a critical analysis of these systemic barriers, the study illustrates their detrimental effects on the academic performance and socio-emotional health of students. Ultimately, the paper argues that true inclusion requires a paradigm shift beyond mere integration; it calls for the alignment of local policies with innovative technological solutions and institutional coordination to ensure an equitable educational experience that benefits the entire academic community.
humanized_abstract: What seems to be the best way of ensuring educational inclusion for children with special educational needs? This paper, which draws on a large bibliographic review of empirical studies and reports, investigates this issue in the context of human rights and current public policy. It identifies, based on the possibilities and constraints of inclusive pedagogy, the main obstacles and areas for improvement, and focuses particularly on Latin America. The research, which was carried out by systematically evaluating the obstacles that hinder the implementation of inclusive teaching at the national level, and analyzing their impact on the academic achievement and social development of students with disabilities, reveals that a whole series of structural obstacles have to be overcome — including lack of resources, unsuitable school premises, and lack of training for teachers — before educational inclusion can become a reality. On the basis of this analysis, this paper sets out to demonstrate that schools need to adopt a very different approach to what can be called the “integration model” and to bring local educational policies into line with the new possibilities arising from the use of new information and communication technologies, and with the inter-institutional coordination required for schools to become truly productive organizations that benefit all their students.
